text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Attending Oncologist: [Attending Physician]
Division of Medical Oncology

---

PATIENT: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Diagnosis: Stage IV (M1c) malignant melanoma, cutaneous primary, BRAF V600E-mutant

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Return visit for oncology follow-up. Patient presents for review of restaging CT results, assessment of response to nivolumab maintenance immunotherapy, and ongoing management of treatment-related endocrinopathy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old female with a history of dysplastic nevus syndrome who was diagnosed with stage IV cutaneous melanoma arising from the back approximately 5 months ago, with metastatic involvement of the liver, bilateral axillary lymph nodes, and subcutaneous tissues of the left flank at presentation. Her primary lesion demonstrated a Breslow thickness of 4.2 mm with ulceration (T4b), and molecular testing confirmed a BRAF V600E mutation. Next-generation sequencing additionally demonstrated co-occurring NF1 loss-of-function mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and a high tumor mutational burden of 42 mutations/Mb. PD-L1 TPS was 30% by 22C3 pharmDx at diagnosis. LDH was significantly elevated at 2.4x the upper limit of normal at presentation.

Given the presence of the BRAF V600E mutation, bulky hepatic disease, and the need for rapid disease control in the setting of elevated LDH and M1c staging, she was initiated on first-line targeted therapy with dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily and trametinib 2 mg once daily. She achieved a marked radiographic response by 8 weeks -- left axillary lymph node had decreased from 4.8 cm to approximately 1.9 cm, and hepatic metastases had shrunk dramatically, with the largest lesion going from 3.2 cm to 1.1 cm. She experienced a transient low-grade fever and nausea in the first weeks of targeted therapy requiring a brief (3-day) dabrafenib hold, with full resolution. At approximately 5 months on therapy, she developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia requiring a temporary trametinib dose reduction to 1.5 mg, with subsequent full-dose resumption.

Unfortunately, restaging at approximately 8 months on dabrafenib/trametinib revealed acquired resistance with disease progression: the left axillary lymph node had grown from 1.8 cm back to 3.4 cm, hepatic lesions had re-enlarged (largest from 1.0 cm to 2.6 cm) with two new hepatic lesions (segment II, 1.5 cm; segment IVa, 1.2 cm), left flank subcutaneous nodule had re-enlarged to 1.4 cm, and a new right inguinal lymph node measuring 1.2 cm had appeared. LDH re-elevated to 285 U/L. Brain MRI performed at the time of progression again showed no intracranial metastases. Dabrafenib/trametinib was discontinued and she was transitioned to second-line immunotherapy with the ipilimumab/nivolumab combination (ipi 3 mg/kg + nivo 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks), planned for 4 induction cycles followed by nivolumab maintenance at 480 mg every 4 weeks.

After completing 2 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab (approximately 5 weeks in), she was hospitalized for grade 3 immune-mediated colitis presenting with 10-12 watery bowel movements daily, crampy abdominal pain, and hematochezia. Sigmoidoscopy confirmed diffuse immune-related colitis. She required IV methylprednisolone and, given lack of improvement at 72 hours, received a single dose of infliximab 5 mg/kg with dramatic symptomatic improvement. Ipilimumab was permanently discontinued given the severity of the toxicity. She completed a prolonged 8-week prednisone taper as an outpatient.

Approximately 3 months after the initial ipilimumab/nivolumab induction, she was also noted to have developed grade 1 immune-mediated hypothyroidism (TSH 8.4 mIU/L, free T4 0.8 ng/dL), consistent with immune-related thyroiditis, and was started on levothyroxine, dose-titrated to 75 mcg daily with TSH normalization.

Once she had fully recovered from the colitis, was off corticosteroids for at least 2 weeks, and had stable thyroid function on hormone replacement, nivolumab maintenance was resumed at 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (approximately 10 weeks after the colitis hospitalization). Today's visit marks approximately 3 months of nivolumab maintenance (approximately 5 months total since the first dose of combination ipilimumab/nivolumab).

Since resuming nivolumab, the patient has been doing extremely well. She reports marked improvement in her energy level and overall functional status -- she has been able to return to her regular activities including full-time work as a high school science teacher. She denies any recurrence of gastrointestinal symptoms and has had no further episodes of diarrhea or abdominal cramping. She notes mild, intermittent fatigue at the end of the day but attributes this to a busy work schedule rather than any medication side effect. She has had no new skin changes, rashes, or joint pains. She denies any dyspnea, cough, or chest discomfort that would raise concern for immune-related pneumonitis. She denies headache, visual changes, or neurological symptoms. She reports good appetite with weight now restored to her pre-illness baseline. She reports cold intolerance has resolved since optimizing levothyroxine dose.

Restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast performed in preparation for today's visit showed substantial improvement in all areas of previously known disease, to be discussed in detail below.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, or significant weight changes; weight is back to baseline. Mild fatigue present but improved.
Head/Eyes/ENT: Denies headache, visual changes, or hearing changes.
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, or leg swelling.
Respiratory: Denies cough, dyspnea, or pleuritic chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, hematochezia, or abdominal pain. Bowel function returned to normal (1-2 formed stools per day). Denies change in stool caliber.
Genitourinary: No complaints.
Musculoskeletal: Denies myalgia or arthralgia.
Dermatologic: Denies new skin lesions, rashes, or pruritus.
Neurological: Denies focal weakness, sensory changes, or cognitive symptoms.
Endocrine: Cold intolerance resolved. No palpitations or heat intolerance.
Psychiatric: Mood improved. No depression or anxiety symptoms endorsed.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY / PROBLEM LIST
1. Stage IV (M1c) cutaneous melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant, currently on nivolumab maintenance immunotherapy
2. Dysplastic nevus syndrome -- multiple prior excisions of atypical nevi over many years
3. Immune-mediated hypothyroidism (secondary to checkpoint inhibitor therapy) -- on levothyroxine replacement
4. History of grade 3 immune-mediated colitis (resolved, on no immunosuppression currently)

SURGICAL HISTORY
- Multiple prior excisions of atypical melanocytic nevi (dysplastic nevus syndrome management)
- Wide local excision of primary melanoma on the back

FAMILY HISTORY
- Maternal uncle: cutaneous melanoma
- No other known first-degree family members with cancer

SOCIAL HISTORY
- High school science teacher, currently working full-time
- Never-smoker
- Minimal alcohol use (occasional wine on weekends)
- Denies illicit drug use
- Of West African and British descent
- Lives with her husband; has two adult children
- Support network strong; husband accompanies her to most appointments

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies
- No known food allergies

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Nivolumab (Opdivo) 480 mg IV every 4 weeks -- maintenance immunotherapy
2. Levothyroxine (Synthroid) 75 mcg orally once daily -- immune-mediated hypothyroidism
3. Vitamin D3 2000 IU orally once daily
4. Multivitamin once daily

(Note: No NSAIDs or immunosuppressive agents. Prednisone taper completed; patient has been off all corticosteroids.)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16, temperature 36.8°C, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 68 kg (baseline restored), height 165 cm, BMI 25.0 kg/m².

General: Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. She is alert and in good spirits. She reports feeling better than she has in over a year.

Head/Eyes/ENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx moist and clear without mucositis. No scleral icterus.

Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No guarding or rebound. Bowel sounds active. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses.

Skin: Multiple well-healed scars from prior nevus excisions on trunk, arms, and back. Primary melanoma excision site on back with well-healed wide local excision scar -- no evidence of local recurrence. No new suspicious pigmented lesions identified on brief skin survey today; formal full-body dermatology examination scheduled separately. No rash. Palms and soles appear normal with no residual hyperkeratosis or erythema (resolved palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia from prior trametinib).

Lymph Nodes:
- Left axilla: A small, soft, mobile lymph node is barely palpable, estimated at approximately 1.5 cm. This is markedly improved from the 5 cm firm, fixed mass present at initial presentation, and continues to decrease from the approximately 2 cm mass noted at the 8-week targeted therapy response visit.
- Right inguinal: No palpable lymphadenopathy. Previously a small right inguinal node was identified on CT imaging at the time of progression on targeted therapy; this appears to have resolved clinically.
- No other peripheral lymphadenopathy (cervical, supraclavicular, right axillary, or femoral nodes not palpable).

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No palmar-plantar changes.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. No focal neurological deficits. Cranial nerves grossly intact.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC 6.8 x10³/μL (normal)
- Hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL (normal for female)
- Platelets 234 x10³/μL (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.0 mEq/L, Creatinine 0.78 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL
- AST 22 U/L (normal), ALT 19 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase 78 U/L (normal)
- Albumin 4.1 g/dL
- Glucose 94 mg/dL

Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH): 148 U/L (within normal limits; ULN 171 U/L)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH 2.4 mIU/L (normal on levothyroxine 75 mcg)
- Free T4 1.2 ng/dL (normal)

Inflammatory Markers:
- CRP 1.2 mg/L (normal)
- ESR not obtained this visit

Urinalysis: Unremarkable.

Note: LFTs entirely normal, representing sustained recovery from prior hepatic metastatic involvement and absence of immune-related hepatitis.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (restaging, reviewed in detail today):

This scan represents a dramatic improvement compared to the nadir imaging at the time of progression on dabrafenib/trametinib and is highly encouraging after resumption of nivolumab. Key findings include:

- Left axillary lymph node: Decreased from 3.4 cm (at progression) to 1.3 cm. This node remains present but is significantly smaller and no longer demonstrates the central necrosis seen at the time of progression.
- Hepatic disease: Markedly improved. The segment VI lesion -- which had grown back to 2.6 cm at progression -- has decreased to 0.9 cm. The segment II lesion that appeared at progression (1.5 cm) is now down to 0.6 cm. The segment IVa lesion that appeared at progression (1.2 cm) is no longer visible on this scan.
- Left flank subcutaneous nodule: Decreased to 0.5 cm from 1.4 cm at progression; barely perceptible.
- Right inguinal lymph node: Decreased to 0.5 cm, not measurable -- effectively resolved.
- No new lesions identified in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.
- LDH normalized to 148 U/L.

Overall, this represents a very favorable response to the immunotherapy regimen, with the vast majority of disease burden substantially reduced or resolved. The remaining small lesions likely represent residual disease or fibrous/inflammatory change. There has been approximately a 55% reduction in measurable disease burden since the start of immunotherapy, and the trajectory continues to be highly favorable.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant cutaneous melanoma with continued excellent response to nivolumab maintenance immunotherapy.

Ms. Osei-Bonsu continues on the nivolumab maintenance phase of her second-line immunotherapy and is doing exceptionally well both clinically and radiographically. Today's restaging CT demonstrates a strong partial response, with all previously progressive lesions now markedly smaller -- hepatic lesions have essentially returned to minimal residual levels, one of the new hepatic lesions that appeared at progression is no longer visible, and the axillary and inguinal lymph node disease and subcutaneous involvement continue to regress. LDH is back to normal, which is a reassuring prognostic indicator in this M1c melanoma patient.

The clinical response achieved here is notable given the complexity of this patient's treatment history. She received a meaningful but truncated course of ipilimumab/nivolumab (only 2 of 4 planned induction cycles) due to grade 3 colitis. Despite this abbreviated induction, she has gone on to mount a robust and deepening immune response on nivolumab monotherapy maintenance. This is consistent with published data showing that even partial induction with the combination can prime durable responses, particularly in patients with favorable immunologic profiles such as high TMB and PD-L1 expression. Her TMB of 42 mutations/Mb is in the high range for melanoma and likely contributed to immunotherapy sensitivity. Her PD-L1 expression of 30% TPS (CPS 55) is also favorable.

Given the pace and depth of the response, there is optimism that she may deepen further toward a complete radiographic response with continued nivolumab. ECOG performance status is back to 0-1, and she is tolerating the single-agent nivolumab well without new immune-related adverse events.

2. Immune-mediated hypothyroidism, stable and well-controlled on levothyroxine.

TSH is 2.4 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily -- within the target range. Free T4 is normal. She is asymptomatic from a thyroid standpoint. No dose adjustment needed at this time. Continue to monitor TSH every 2-3 months per standard protocol for immune checkpoint inhibitor-related thyroid disease.

3. History of grade 3 immune-mediated colitis, resolved.

Bowel function is normal and has remained stable since completing the prednisone taper and infliximab administration. CRP is normal. No symptoms to suggest recurrence of colitis. Given that ipilimumab has been permanently discontinued, the risk of recurrent severe colitis with single-agent nivolumab is substantially lower, though she will remain on vigilant monitoring.

4. Dysplastic nevus syndrome -- surveillance ongoing.

Patient has a longstanding history of dysplastic nevus syndrome with multiple prior atypical nevus excisions and a positive family history of melanoma (maternal uncle). With her primary cutaneous melanoma now being managed systemically, ongoing dermatologic surveillance remains important for detection of new primary lesions. Dermatology appointment confirmed at approximately 6-month intervals.

---

PLAN

1. Continue nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks. Patient has tolerated the last 3 infusions without significant infusion-related reactions or new immune-related adverse events. Pre-medications not routinely required at this point. She will receive her next infusion as scheduled.

2. Repeat restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months. Given the excellent trajectory, continue 3-month surveillance intervals. If she achieves a complete radiographic response, we will begin discussions about surveillance intervals and the optimal duration of nivolumab therapy -- per emerging evidence, patients achieving a complete response on anti-PD-1 maintenance may be candidates for elective treatment discontinuation after approximately 2 years total immunotherapy exposure; we will revisit this in detail when/if complete response is confirmed.

3. Brain MRI: Not urgently indicated at this time given she has had two brain MRIs both showing no intracranial disease, and she has no neurological symptoms. Plan to obtain brain MRI at the time of next significant restaging, approximately 6 months from now, per institutional surveillance protocol.

4. Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily. Repeat TSH and free T4 in 3 months. No dose change indicated today.

5. Dermatology follow-up: Patient has an appointment scheduled in approximately 6 months for whole-body skin examination (dysplastic nevus syndrome surveillance). Remind patient to perform monthly self-skin examinations and report any new or changing lesions promptly given her personal and family history.

6. Colitis monitoring: Reinforce with patient to report any increase in bowel movements, diarrhea, rectal bleeding, or abdominal cramping immediately. She understands this well given her prior hospitalization. Stool diary not currently required given normal bowel function, but patient will call with any GI concerns.

7. Immune-related adverse event counseling: Continued counseling regarding signs and symptoms of other immune-related toxicities. Specifically discussed monitoring for:
   - Pneumonitis: dyspnea, new cough, or hypoxia (relatively uncommon with nivolumab monotherapy but possible)
   - Hepatitis: jaundice, right upper quadrant pain (LFTs normal today, continue to monitor)
   - Adrenal insufficiency: profound fatigue, orthostasis (low probability but relevant in setting of prior corticosteroid use and ongoing immune therapy)
   - Rheumatologic toxicity: new joint swelling, arthralgia, or muscle aches
   - Ocular toxicity: blurred vision, photophobia (rare)
   Patient given contact information for urgent triage line and instructed to call prior to starting any new medications including antibiotics or corticosteroids.

8. Labs at next visit: CBC, CMP, LDH, TSH. Consider morning cortisol and ACTH stimulation testing if fatigue worsens or hypotension develops.

9. Psychological and social support: Patient is coping well and has returned to work full-time. She expressed optimism about her prognosis given the imaging results. Discussed that durable responses with immunotherapy in melanoma can be long-lasting, particularly when a deep response is achieved. No formal psychology referral indicated at this time; patient has strong family support. Will continue to assess at follow-up visits.

10. Return to clinic in approximately 4 weeks for next nivolumab infusion and interim clinical assessment, then full restaging visit in approximately 3 months.

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes, including review of imaging, laboratory results, counseling, and treatment planning. Greater than 50% of encounter time was spent on counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist]
[Dictated and finalized on date of service]
